title: Pointers, arrays and arithmetic

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Pointers, arrays and arithmetic; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Within one array object, pointer addition advances by elements and comparisons can include the one-past pointer, while dereference remains limited to actual elements.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A loop advances int *p from values to values + count, stopping before p equals the one-past endpoint. Your goal is to rewrite and trace equivalent bounded array and pointer traversals. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: One-dimensional arrays; Parameters and return values; Addresses and dereference. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Within one array object, pointer addition advances by elements and comparisons can include the one-past pointer, while dereference remains limited to actual elements. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Arithmetic across unrelated objects is not defined, one-past may not be dereferenced, and array-to-pointer conversion loses total length information. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Pointer arithmetic counts bytes for every pointer type and can traverse freely between separately allocated objects.

includes: Relate array conversion, indexing and pointer arithmetic within one array object.
Rewrite and trace equivalent bounded array and pointer traversals.
excludes: Arithmetic across unrelated objects is not defined, one-past may not be dereferenced, and array-to-pointer conversion loses total length information.

outcomes: Rewrite and trace equivalent bounded array and pointer traversals.

examples.title: Pointers, arrays and arithmetic: defined case
examples.situation: A loop advances int *p from values to values + count, stopping before p equals the one-past endpoint.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A loop advances int *p from values to values + count, stopping before p equals the one-past endpoint.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Pointer arithmetic is scaled by the pointed-to type and is defined only within one array object plus its one-past position.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Pointer arithmetic is scaled by the pointed-to type and is defined only within one array object plus its one-past position.

examples.title: Pointers, arrays and arithmetic: boundary case
examples.situation: Subtracting two pointers into the same array yields an element distance, but subtracting pointers into separate arrays is not a portable distance.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Subtracting two pointers into the same array yields an element distance, but subtracting pointers into separate arrays is not a portable distance.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Arithmetic across unrelated objects is not defined, one-past may not be dereferenced, and array-to-pointer conversion loses total length information.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Pointer arithmetic counts bytes for every pointer type and can traverse freely between separately allocated objects.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Rewrite and trace equivalent bounded array and pointer traversals.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Pointer arithmetic counts bytes for every pointer type and can traverse freely between separately allocated objects.
misconceptions.correction: Pointer arithmetic is scaled by the pointed-to type and is defined only within one array object plus its one-past position.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Pointer arithmetic counts bytes for every pointer type and can traverse freely between separately allocated objects.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Pointers, arrays and arithmetic: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
